speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 16, 1996. The survivor is Jeremiah Kirschenbaum, K-I-R-S-C-H-E-N-B-A-U-M, same name at birth, Kirschenbaum. The interviewer is Rivka Wakslak in Brooklyn, New York, the United States of America. The language is English and Yiddish. This is Jeremiah Kirschenbaum. I am Rivka Wakslak. The date is December 16, 1996. The language is English and Yiddish. Good afternoon. Could you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jeremiah Kirschenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. K-I-R-S-C-H-E-N-B-A-U-M. Jeremiah, J-E-R-E-M-I-A-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, was that the name you were given when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am born on January 26, '12, 1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now, Mr. Kirschenbaum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now, Mr. Kirschenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Palew-- Padew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Padew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Padew is-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the state was Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a town is Padew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Padew was a town, there was maybe 600 families. Now, in these 600 families was few, maybe 40 Jewish and, and German, too. No, maybe 40 families were German. The German was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of the families were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish. And only a few families were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 40, 40 family, a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people do in the town of Padew for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near what town was Padew?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a lot, they got small farms. Small farms, not big.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a small farm, too. And we got a business, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your family? What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name, Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Yakov.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he worked most on the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he also had a store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say he also had a store.

speaker: interviewee
text: A store. Most I was in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grocery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the things for the grocery come from? Did they come from your farm? That you sold?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. We bought in a bigger city, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is a-- my grandfather, he got a bigger farm. In business, too. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got brothers and sisters, two brothers and one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest, I think, passed away. After this, I was. Well, I'm not a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And who came after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: After it was Meilech. Meilech was, maybe, 10 years younger than me. Why? My father was in the-- in first war, in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was serving the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what capacity? What branch of the army was he in? Do you know the branch of the army? Do you know anything about his army career? About his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I know only that we got in the government in Polish to-- to can sell cigarettes. In Poland, you must have a special, a special this for cigarettes. Here, everybody can have cigarettes. In Poland, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to have permission?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, permission, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: To sell--

speaker: interviewee
text: For this, while he was in the army, he got, maybe, it's 20% what-- invalid, no. And for this reason, he got this business.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was injured in the First World War, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be something, because that's the-- he got the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have some kind of disability that you could see? Was he disabled?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he got something because of his service. He got the permit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Normal, only the permit to sell cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a valuable thing, the permit to sell?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The cigarettes bring a different business in place. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the grocery, of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: It helped the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. But not everybody got the permission to sell cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you sell in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. Flour, sugar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the street where you lived in Padew? How big was that street? How many houses on that street?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, the, the place was, maybe, 600 families. Now, between the 600 was German, maybe 40, 50. I don't know. And Jewish, the, the same amount.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but the street you lived on-- the street, do you remember what the street looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street was a big street. The same how the street is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the store in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the store that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the house, and the store was in a small room in the house, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms were in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got three, and the store, four.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the whole house?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big were the rooms in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I cannot explain. No, we got a, a stable, too. A stable means for, for cows, for this. We got-- this was-- this was separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the rooms big?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bigger than here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have running water in your town, in the house? Did you have water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we got separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Separate place to take the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far from the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not far.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who brought the water in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody got a chance, he brought the water. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to the school in, in German school, in Deustch. Now I forgot. I write-- no, how you say? Not English words. No. I remember how this. Now I forgot. When I make an 'm,' I make--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the German schoo.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go to the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was friends with the German. Now, after this, it was a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning, when you were a young child, the Germans were friendly to the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. We got friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had German boys who were friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was close. We went to the school across the street there, the Germans got a church, a German church.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you learn in school, in that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deutsch. Now, I think, after two-- the second class, we start Polish, too. No, I don't went too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a yeshiva, to a cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now this is, I was in Recz Cheder, the city of Recz. I was in Recz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go to Recz?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was older.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as a small child, you didn't go to a different school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grades did you go to this German school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I start. After this, I went to the-- maybe two, three classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to, to Recz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family sent you away from home to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I got-- no. I got an uncle in Recz. I was with him. And there I went to the yeshiva in Recz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you went to Recz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you went to Recz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I was 15, 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe-- was-- were the Jewish people in your town, were they religious Jewish people? Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Most, most, most. Maybe somebody was in the house, no. No, in street is everybody religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and can you tell me what Shabbos was like in Padew?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to daven, to the shul. And after this, at home, we would come back. We got a, a mikvah with water, yeah. It was not a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went to the mikvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: The men went every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: The men went--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not every day. Maybe Friday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they use the mikvah for bathing, also, or did they bathe at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take baths at home or in the mikvah? The bathing? People bathed--did they take baths at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Bathing in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the house. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Did you help at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help your mother at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was in-- most, I was in store. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- do you remember what Shabbos was like, what your mother made to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eat? The same [INAUDIBLE] Shabbos, a cholent, we'd say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the cholent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Better than here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fleysh. [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she put in beans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe beans. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, in my house, only a potato. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yontif? What about Pesach? What was Pesach like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we baked matzos by himself. We don't [INAUDIBLE] for matzos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you bake them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the places with the [INAUDIBLE]. And one, one family helped another family. And we baked the matzos.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many matzos--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] kosher matzos.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many matzos did you have to bake for Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, so we baked a lot of matzos. Why? My mother, for the stomach, was the matzos better. [INAUDIBLE] was the matzos. She was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: She wasn't well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The matzos was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: She felt that was better.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father successful in business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, the father was not too much in business, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family comfortable? Did they live comfortably? Or was it difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we got to everything that we need, need for [INAUDIBLE] we got.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other people in Padew were they also comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. A lot got whiskey-- a permit for whisky. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were relations--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got on our permit for cigarettes, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What were relations like between the Jews and the non-Jews, the Polish Christians in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we lived very good. We don't know one another. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ah, when Hitler come, comes, the German was no more friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did you read the papers, the newspapers in Yiddish in those days before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. We got--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which newspapers did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember reading before the war about-- were things changing for the Jews in Poland? Did things get worse before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they start getting worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before Hitler had started to, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did things change when Pilsudski died? When Pilsudski died, did things get worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a friend. And after this, it was a little bit sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I-- It was a little bit in the government, a change. Pilsudski went away, and it was not a, a better man than him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who came into power afterwards that your-- in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not [INAUDIBLE], I mean in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did things change in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did things change in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much. Not so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were different policies put into place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have different policies to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It started, the policy started when Hitler come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Did the-- did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a law that the horses to go the different way. This and-- this is not--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of law was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a law that who make gates must be in left side, the right side. It--

speaker: interviewer
text: These were laws that made a difference between Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The law, it was for everybody. Was this a hardship, this law?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make life harder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who don't like it, like it was-- the no law was special for Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: No laws that were against the Jews beforehand?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Christians boycott the Jewish stores? Did they stop going to Jewish stores?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, after it started. The biggest store. No. The Polish people want them-- let them eat. He writes on the book. He give you on credit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have good relationships with your customers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your customers had good relations with the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any anti-Semitic people in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In every place you can have anti-Semitism, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He don't like the, the meat, but that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then if you didn't like the meat, what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's there to do? Most, most Polish people was friendly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean before Hitler. Did anybody from your town want to go to Israel, to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Palestine was out of question.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the question?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rebbes was against.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which rebbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father go to a rebbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which rebbe did he go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: The [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever take you to the rebbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? No. I was by myself in, in, in town of rebbe. The [? Stuchiner ?]. You got here a [INAUDIBLE], too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the rebbe tell you? Did you ever ask the rebbe [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- well, we went to a rebbe. That's all. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers and your sister, what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother, Elimelich, he learned in the yeshiva in Tarnow. And the younger was at home with us. And the sister was, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she help at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I remember when the war-- out in '39. This was the first city that was-- [INAUDIBLE]. Most [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mielec.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Mielec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mielec.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is Mielec.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you know about Mielec?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was shown on, on the road, caravans and Jewish people--

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right away after the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. After this. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after this, the Germans took us to work by the roads, to fix up the roads. We worked. And after this, took us from Padew to Baranow. Baranow and that. The Judenrat gave us rooms. After this, the Judenrat took me to the lager. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I [INAUDIBLE]. And we was in lager. And one time, for money, I went for Shabbos to the town. We don't care [INAUDIBLE]. This time, was all the Jewish took to market. And my mother took, took me and go back to the lager, to the lager. He took me between the-- this was a small place. And she went home to Baranow, and I went in the, the lager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother want in this way. Got the mind, maybe, there will be better.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when you, you say that your family was taken from Padew to Baranow. When did that happen, do you know? When were you taken from Padew to Baranow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know. Maybe I got a piece of paper. Maybe I got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what they told you when they took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just, just-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no. They took us '40-- must be on '42, 1942 when it started. March 1942 I went to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember traveling from Padew to Baranow? Do you remember when you went from Padew to Baranow? Do you remember the traveling when they took you from Padew to Baranow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was, in this time, you can find Polish people, they took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to go by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Polish people with the horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: They forced you to go to Baranow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were forced to go to Baranow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family take things with them? Did you pack up stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you can take on this?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take some extra clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothing. Maybe to eat, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you went in Padew-- in Baranow from Padew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. After this I was not long in Baranow. They took me to, to, to the [INAUDIBLE]. The Judenrat sent, sent us. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was in in [INAUDIBLE] this summer, 1943. What-- can you tell me a little bit more about-- how long were you in Baranow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not--

speaker: interviewer
text: A week?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: A month?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay while you were in Baranow, can you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat give us rooms. Everybody who got rooms, he got a place to take a family, he must take.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which family you were given to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just we was [INAUDIBLE] Kirschenbaum. He was, maybe, related to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the, the place where you were placed, the place where you stayed when you were in Baranow, the Kirschenbaum house?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] , one room, small room.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were you in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was one and then-- [INAUDIBLE] we was five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have food there for you? Did they give you food in Baranow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe we got by himself, food. This Kirschenbaum was not a rich man, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to get-- take care of your own food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not allowed-- no, we went through a village where we know the Polish people would give us something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay for this food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. That's-- we, we got-- we got money but this-- when he, he don't want to give, he must not give. No, it was a-- the same, what I signed, but the, the papers in here, in America. Also, we went to [INAUDIBLE] in the lager, he give us to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this? What was the name of this Pole, this Polish person who gave you to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, you saw this paper, I think. What I signed in Baranow council.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He give us [INAUDIBLE]. We eat, we, we sleep, and the next day, we went back in the nighttime to the-- to the concentration lager. Well, we got no place where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a pause here for a tape change.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come back in a minute.
